summary: The Sprout Social dashboard presents a dense but functional overview of user activity. While the layout is generally clear, significant usability and accessibility issues detract from the experience, including ambiguous icon-only navigation, inconsistent interactive element styling, and several instances of low-contrast text that fail WCAG 2.2 standards, hindering data comprehension.

issues.title: Ambiguous Icon-Only Buttons in Utility Navigation
issues.problem: The vertical navigation bar on the right uses icons without text labels. Icons such as the plus symbol, link symbol, or group of people are not universally understood, forcing users into a trial-and-error process to discover their function.
issues.critical_reason: Relying solely on icons increases cognitive load and creates a Gulf of Execution where users cannot determine how to perform their desired action. This violates the principle of clarity, making the interface less discoverable.
issues.recommendation: Add a tooltip that appears on hover and focus for each icon, clearly stating its function (e.g., "Compose Post", "Notifications"). Ensure these tooltips are fully accessible to screen reader and keyboard-only users.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Signifier: visible cues must indicate where to act

issues.title: Low Contrast for Engagement Metrics
issues.location: Engagement counts under each recent post
issues.problem: The grey text for the engagement counts ('7' and '5') has insufficient contrast against the white card background. This makes the numbers, a key performance metric, difficult to read for users, especially those with moderate visual impairments.
issues.critical_reason: Text with insufficient contrast excludes users with visual disabilities and strains the eyes of all users, directly violating WCAG 2.2 guidelines for web accessibility. For a data-heavy application, clear visibility of metrics is paramount.
issues.recommendation: Change the color of the engagement count text to a darker shade of grey or black (e.g., #595959 or darker) to ensure it meets the minimum WCAG AA contrast ratio of 4.5:1 against the background.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): normal text requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio

issues.title: Ambiguous and Low-Contrast Placeholder Element
issues.location: Grey box with the letter 'A' in recent posts
issues.problem: A grey box containing a faint letter 'A' appears in the recent posts. Its purpose is completely unclear, and the letter itself has extremely low contrast against its grey background, making it unreadable and failing accessibility standards.
issues.critical_reason: This element violates the principle of clarity, introducing visual noise and increasing cognitive load as users try to decipher its meaning. The poor contrast also fails WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 1.4.3.
issues.recommendation: Replace this ambiguous element with a meaningful icon that includes a descriptive tooltip, or remove it if it provides no value. Any text or graphical object used must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 for text or 3:1 for graphics.

issues.title: Inconsistent Styling for Interactive Elements
issues.location: Various buttons and links across the dashboard
issues.problem: Clickable elements have inconsistent visual treatments. For example, "Start my subscription" is a solid button, "Post Performance Report" is an outline button, "Open Tasks" is a text link, and "You have new messages!" is a button styled as a status banner.
issues.critical_reason: Inconsistent design patterns break the principle of learnability and create confusion about what is clickable. This forces users to relearn the UI's interaction language in different contexts, increasing cognitive load.
issues.recommendation: Establish and consistently apply a clear visual hierarchy for interactive elements. Use solid buttons for primary actions, outlined or secondary-color buttons for secondary actions, and a consistent link style for navigation.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Affordance: element must communicate how it is used
